refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a performance target and (2) sustaining it over time. The refined version separates these concerns while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing how availability is achieved (e.g., redundancy, predictive maintenance, rapid repair) and focuses on the measurable outcome in the problem domain. The phrase 'achieve and maintain' captures both the initial attainment and sustained performance without introducing implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, scheduled vs. unscheduled maintenance events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Downtime) / Total Time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that the measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold for the full evaluation window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall achieve the customer-defined operational availability target at the start of the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall sustain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target throughout the agreed evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant critical subsystems (power, control, sensing) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity.
DP2: Design modular architecture with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces to enable rapid replacement of failed units without system shutdown.
DP3: Establish predictive maintenance framework using condition monitoring and degradation models to detect and address faults before they cause unavailability.
DP4: Implement graceful degradation strategy where non-critical functions are shed under fault conditions to preserve core operational capability.
DP5: Deploy distributed control with local autonomy and inter-node synchronization to reduce dependency on centralized decision points.
DP6: Establish comprehensive fault detection and isolation (FDIA) subsystem with multi-layer diagnostics (hardware, firmware, application) to enable rapid root-cause identification.
DP7: Implement configuration management and automated recovery procedures (restart, reset, reconfiguration) to restore nominal operation without manual intervention.
DP8: Design system with built-in test (BIST) and health monitoring that continuously validates subsystem state and triggers corrective actions when thresholds are breached.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP3 + DP6 + DP7 (integrated approach): Achieve availability through (1) redundancy with automatic failover for critical paths, (2) predictive maintenance to prevent faults, (3) rapid fault detection and isolation, and (4) automated recovery procedures. This combination addresses both fault prevention (DP3) and fault tolerance (DP1, DP6, DP7), reducing mean time to recovery (MTTR) and mean time between failures (MTBF).

potential_sources: DP1 (redundancy) and DP3 (predictive maintenance) are loosely coupled: redundancy masks faults while predictive maintenance prevents them—both are necessary but operate on different timescales. Risk: over-reliance on redundancy may defer maintenance decisions.

DP6 (FDIA) and DP7 (automated recovery) are tightly coupled: FDIA must provide sufficient diagnostic resolution to enable targeted recovery actions. Risk: poor diagnostic granularity will trigger overly broad recovery (e.g., full system restart instead of component reset).

DP1 and DP7 interact: automated recovery procedures must account for redundancy state (e.g., do not restart a unit if its redundant partner is already failed). Risk: race conditions or cascading failures if recovery logic does not respect redundancy topology.
DP3 (predictive maintenance) depends on DP8 (health monitoring) for data; if monitoring is absent or unreliable, predictive maintenance cannot function. This is a hidden dependency.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP set is not orthogonal but is acceptable because couplings are logical (not arbitrary) and can be managed through clear interface contracts. The main risk is that DP1 (redundancy) may mask symptoms that DP3 (predictive maintenance) needs to detect; mitigation requires that health monitoring (DP8) operates independently of redundancy state.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined target availability (≥0.97) is achievable with the proposed DP set given realistic component failure rates and repair/replacement lead times. RISK: If component MTBF is very low or spare parts availability is poor, even redundancy may not meet target. Mitigation: conduct FMEA and availability modeling early.

ASSUMPTION: Predictive maintenance (DP3) can be implemented with sufficient accuracy to prevent most faults before they cause unavailability. RISK: Condition monitoring sensors may be unreliable, or degradation models may not capture all failure modes. Mitigation: validate predictive models against historical failure data; include conservative safety margins.

ASSUMPTION: Automated recovery procedures (DP7) will succeed without manual intervention in ≥95% of fault scenarios. RISK: Some faults may require human judgment or physical intervention (e.g., mechanical jam, environmental hazard). Mitigation: define clear boundaries between automated and manual recovery; ensure safety interlocks prevent unsafe automated actions.

ASSUMPTION: Redundant subsystems can be kept synchronized and will not diverge in state or behavior. RISK: Synchronization failures or Byzantine faults (where a redundant unit fails in a way that corrupts shared state) could reduce availability below target. Mitigation: implement voting or consensus logic; design for fail-safe (not fail-operational) behavior.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is long enough (weeks to months) to capture representative fault scenarios and validate availability claims. RISK: Short evaluation periods may not expose rare failure modes or seasonal effects. Mitigation: extend evaluation period or use accelerated life testing.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies do not impose constraints that conflict with automated recovery (e.g., no automatic restart during certain hours). RISK: Safety or operational constraints may force manual intervention, reducing effective availability. Mitigation: align DP7 (automated recovery) with safety and operational policies early in design.

ASSUMPTION: System interfaces and integration points are well-defined and stable. RISK: If interfaces change frequently or are poorly documented, redundancy and failover logic may fail. Mitigation: establish interface control documents and version management.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine the selected DP set to explicitly include DP8 (health monitoring) as a foundational element, since DP3 (predictive maintenance) and DP6 (FDIA) both depend on it. Revise the FR to clarify whether availability is measured as 'system-level uptime' (any core function available) or 'full-capability uptime' (all functions available). This distinction affects the design of graceful degradation (DP4) and redundancy scope (DP1). Additionally, add a secondary FR to address 'mean time to recovery' (MTTR) as an explicit design target, since MTTR is a key lever for availability: Availability ≈ MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR). Finally, conduct a preliminary FMEA to identify which subsystems are critical for availability and should be prioritized for redundancy and predictive maintenance investment.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for fault detection, isolation, predictive maintenance monitoring, automated recovery procedures, and redundancy coordination across the system. Owns the logic for health assessment, failure prediction, failover orchestration, and recovery automation.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives continuous health and operational data from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to enable fault detection, predictive maintenance analysis, and system state assessment.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Broadcasts fault detection results, isolation directives, recovery status, and safety-critical alerts to Vehicle and Teleoperation for immediate action.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives task context from NGHE Solution; sends recovery readiness status and failover completion confirmations to coordinate mission continuity.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Uses network for redundancy coordination, remote diagnostics, and failover signaling across distributed system elements.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Safety Services is the natural owner of fault detection, isolation, predictive maintenance, and recovery automation. All four DP components (redundancy failover, predictive maintenance, fault detection/isolation, automated recovery) align with safety-critical system management. Minor diffusion: Vehicle and Teleoperation must implement redundancy switching and recovery actions, but orchestration logic remains centralized in Safety Services.

Poor fit. Vehicle is a physical/operational platform, not a system-level availability manager. Predictive maintenance, fault detection, and recovery orchestration would diffuse across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution. Redundancy failover logic would be scattered. High coupling and low cohesion.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a control interface, not a fault management system. Predictive maintenance and automated recovery procedures are not teleoperation concerns. Fault detection and failover would require tight coupling to Vehicle and Safety Services. Significant diffusion.

Poor fit. NGHE Solution is a mission/task orchestration layer. Fault detection, predictive maintenance, and automated recovery are operational concerns that belong at a lower level. Placing availability logic here creates tight coupling to Vehicle and Teleoperation, and violates separation of concerns.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the most specific and appropriate boundary for the integrated DP. It is explicitly designed to manage system health, safety-critical faults, and recovery—all core to the combined DP. The module has clear interfaces (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert out, Tasking and Network Link for coordination) and can orchestrate redundancy, predictive maintenance, fault isolation, and automated recovery without diffusing logic across multiple subsystems.
containment_risks: Risk: Vehicle and Teleoperation must implement redundancy switching and recovery actions. Mitigation: Define clear SafetyAlert and Tasking interface contracts so Safety Services orchestrates but does not own execution.
Risk: Predictive maintenance requires deep knowledge of Vehicle subsystem health signatures. Mitigation: Safety Services consumes Telemetry; Vehicle provides health data in standardized format.
Risk: Network Link availability is critical for distributed failover coordination. Mitigation: Safety Services must handle Network Link failures gracefully; design local failover logic as fallback.
Risk: Automated recovery procedures may conflict with ongoing Teleoperation commands. Mitigation: Safety Services has priority; Teleoperation must respect SafetyAlert directives.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Availability is a measurable, quantifiable attribute (≥0.97 over evaluation period). Safety Services module owns the instrumentation (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert out) and orchestration logic needed to track and report availability metrics. Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems provide health data and execute recovery actions; Safety Services aggregates and analyzes. Verification can be performed through continuous monitoring dashboards, post-mission availability reports, and controlled fault injection testing.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR allocation to DP is explicit: four integrated mechanisms (redundancy failover, predictive maintenance, fault detection/isolation, automated recovery) directly address availability. DP-to-IM allocation is well-justified: Safety Services is the natural owner of fault management and recovery orchestration. Interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, Network Link) define clear data and control flows. Responsibility boundaries are unambiguous: Safety Services orchestrates; Vehicle and Teleoperation execute.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP is logically sound and complete. The four-part integrated approach (DP1: redundancy+failover, DP3: predictive maintenance, DP6: fault detection+isolation, DP7: automated recovery) directly and comprehensively addresses the FR requirement to achieve and maintain availability ≥0.97. The combination targets both fault prevention (DP3) and fault tolerance (DP1, DP6, DP7), reducing MTTR and MTBF—the two primary levers for availability. No logical gaps identified. DP is traceable to FR and measurable against acceptance criteria.

diffusion_risks: Minor diffusion: Vehicle and Teleoperation must implement redundancy switching and recovery actions (e.g., motor failover, control mode transitions). Mitigation: Safety Services sends orchestration directives via SafetyAlert interface; Vehicle and Teleoperation implement standardized recovery action handlers. Define interface contracts (e.g., 'SafetyAlert.FailoverCommand' → Vehicle.executeFailover()) to prevent logic scatter.

Moderate diffusion risk: Predictive maintenance requires Vehicle subsystem to expose health signatures (bearing vibration, thermal trends, power consumption patterns) in standardized Telemetry format. If Vehicle does not provide structured health data, Safety Services cannot perform predictive analysis. Mitigation: Establish Telemetry schema and health data standards; Vehicle owns data collection and formatting; Safety Services owns analysis and prediction logic.

Critical dependency: Network Link availability is essential for distributed failover coordination and remote diagnostics. If Network Link fails, Safety Services cannot coordinate failover across geographically separated Vehicle and Teleoperation nodes. Mitigation: Design local failover logic as fallback (e.g., Vehicle can detect local faults and initiate local recovery without waiting for Safety Services command). Document Network Link failure modes and recovery procedures.

Operational coupling: Automated recovery procedures (DP7) may conflict with ongoing Teleoperation commands (e.g., operator commands vehicle to move; Safety Services detects fault and initiates recovery shutdown). Mitigation: Establish priority rules in SafetyAlert interface; Safety Services has override authority; Teleoperation must respect SafetyAlert directives and suspend operator commands during recovery.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment. Safety Services achieves 0.92 containment score with only 0.08 diffusion. The module is purpose-built for fault management, health monitoring, and recovery orchestration. All four DP components (redundancy failover, predictive maintenance, fault detection/isolation, automated recovery) align with Safety Services' core responsibility. Interfaces are well-defined: Telemetry (in) provides health data; SafetyAlert (out) broadcasts directives; Tasking (inout) coordinates mission context; Network Link (inout) enables distributed coordination. Alternative candidates (Vehicle, Teleoperation, NGHE Solution) show poor fit (0.35–0.55 containment) due to misalignment with their primary responsibilities. Safety Services is the correct architectural choice.

recommended_revisions: Formalize Telemetry schema: Define standardized health data format (e.g., JSON schema or protobuf) that Vehicle and Teleoperation must emit. Include fields for fault codes, sensor readings, subsystem status, and timestamps. Safety Services consumes this schema for predictive maintenance analysis.

Define SafetyAlert interface contract: Specify command types (e.g., FailoverCommand, IsolationDirective, RecoveryStatus), priority levels, and expected response times. Vehicle and Teleoperation must implement handlers for each command type. Document timeout and retry behavior.

Establish Network Link failure modes and fallback procedures: Document scenarios where Network Link is unavailable (e.g., communication latency >threshold, packet loss >threshold). Define local failover logic that Vehicle can execute autonomously without Safety Services coordination. Specify conditions under which local failover is triggered and how Safety Services regains coordination when Network Link recovers.

Create priority and conflict resolution rules: Document how Safety Services SafetyAlert directives override Teleoperation operator commands. Define grace periods for operator command cancellation and recovery action initiation. Specify how Teleoperation is notified of recovery status and when operator control is restored.

Add acceptance criteria for DP components: Specify measurable targets for each DP: (DP1) Redundancy failover latency <X ms; (DP3) Predictive maintenance false positive rate <Y%; (DP6) Fault detection latency <Z ms; (DP7) Automated recovery success rate >W%. These enable verification of DP effectiveness and contribution to overall availability target (≥0.97).

Document Safety Services module interfaces in detail: Create interface control documents (ICDs) for Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, and Network Link. Include data types, message formats, timing constraints, and error handling. Ensure Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution teams have clear, unambiguous specifications.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The DP is logically sound, the IM allocation is well-justified, and verification is feasible. However, the gate results expose critical dependencies and diffusion risks that require decomposition of the current FR into sub-level FRs to ensure architectural clarity, interface contracts, and fallback logic are explicitly designed and owned. The recommended revisions point to four distinct sub-problems: (1) standardized health data schema and collection, (2) SafetyAlert command interface and priority rules, (3) Network Link failure modes and local failover logic, and (4) conflict resolution between automated recovery and operator commands. These are not minor implementation details—they are architectural decisions that must be elevated to the FR-DP-IM hierarchy to ensure proper ownership, verification, and risk mitigation. Given high safety criticality, decomposition is warranted to make these dependencies explicit and traceable.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-2.1: Provide standardized health data collection and telemetry schema that enables Safety Services to perform predictive maintenance and fault detection across Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems.

FR-2.2: Define and enforce SafetyAlert command interface with priority rules, timeout behavior, and conflict resolution to ensure automated recovery directives override operator commands and are executed reliably.
FR-2.3: Enable local autonomous failover and recovery logic in Vehicle subsystem as fallback when Network Link is unavailable, with automatic re-synchronization when Network Link recovers.
FR-2.4: Coordinate automated recovery procedures with Teleoperation operator commands to prevent conflicts, with clear priority rules, grace periods, and operator notification of recovery status.

revise.why: The current DP (four integrated mechanisms: redundancy failover, predictive maintenance, fault detection/isolation, automated recovery) is logically sound but lacks explicit sub-DPs for the four architectural dependencies identified in the gate results. Decomposing the FR into sub-FRs will force corresponding sub-DPs that address: (1) Telemetry schema design and standardization, (2) SafetyAlert interface contract and priority logic, (3) local failover logic and Network Link recovery procedures, and (4) operator command conflict resolution. This ensures each sub-DP is traceable to a sub-FR, owned by a clear IM, and verifiable against acceptance criteria. The current DP is correct but incomplete at the architectural level.

assumptions: Vehicle subsystem can expose structured health data (bearing vibration, thermal trends, power consumption) in standardized Telemetry format without major redesign.
Network Link failures are detectable and can trigger local failover logic in Vehicle subsystem with acceptable latency (<X ms).
Teleoperation operator commands can be suspended or overridden by Safety Services SafetyAlert directives without violating mission objectives or safety constraints.
Safety Services has sufficient computational capacity to perform real-time predictive maintenance analysis and fault detection on continuous Telemetry streams from Vehicle and Teleoperation.
Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems can implement standardized recovery action handlers (e.g., executeFailover(), isolateFault(), resumeOperation()) without architectural conflicts.
open_risks: Telemetry schema mismatch: If Vehicle and Teleoperation emit health data in incompatible formats, Safety Services cannot aggregate and analyze. Risk level: High. Mitigation: Establish schema early in design; enforce via interface control documents (ICDs).

Network Link single point of failure: Distributed failover coordination depends on Network Link availability. If Network Link fails, Safety Services cannot orchestrate failover across geographically separated nodes. Risk level: Critical. Mitigation: Design local failover logic as fallback; document Network Link failure modes and recovery procedures in sub-FR-2.3.

Operator-recovery conflict: Automated recovery procedures may conflict with ongoing Teleoperation commands, causing mission interruption or safety violations. Risk level: High. Mitigation: Establish priority rules and conflict resolution logic in sub-FR-2.4; define grace periods and operator notification procedures.

Predictive maintenance false positives: Predictive maintenance (DP3) may trigger unnecessary recovery actions, reducing availability and operator confidence. Risk level: Moderate. Mitigation: Set acceptance criterion for false positive rate (<Y%); validate predictive models against historical data; implement operator override with audit trail.

Local failover logic divergence: If Vehicle implements local failover logic independently, it may diverge from Safety Services orchestration logic, causing inconsistent behavior. Risk level: Moderate. Mitigation: Define local failover logic as part of sub-FR-2.3; ensure consistency with Safety Services orchestration via interface contracts.
evidence_needed: Telemetry schema specification (JSON schema or protobuf) with examples of health data from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems.
SafetyAlert interface control document (ICD) with command types, priority levels, timeout behavior, and expected response times.
Network Link failure mode analysis (FMEA) with detection mechanisms, local failover triggers, and re-synchronization procedures.
Operator command conflict resolution matrix: priority rules, grace periods, and notification procedures for recovery actions.
Acceptance criteria for each DP component: redundancy failover latency, predictive maintenance false positive rate, fault detection latency, automated recovery success rate.
Proof-of-concept or prototype demonstrating local failover logic in Vehicle subsystem without Safety Services coordination.
Historical availability data or simulation results showing how the four DP mechanisms combine to achieve ≥0.97 availability target.
Interface control documents (ICDs) for Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, and Network Link with data types, message formats, timing constraints, and error handling.
